name: Participant Pre-Admission Screening Stringency
consequences: Stricter screening (additional overnight PSG, sleep diaries, collateral interviews) improves event-yield confidence and reduces the 20% unproductive-admission ceiling risk, but delays recruitment and increases per-participant enrollment costs. Conversely, lighter screening accelerates admission but risks admitting participants unlikely to produce captured events within the 10-week window, consuming facility capacity and budget with minimal data yield.
options: Require two nights of baseline PSG at referring clinic prior to admission, with event-yield prediction modeling and enrollment only if modeled probability exceeds 60%, accepting slower early recruitment in exchange for higher productive-stay rates.

Conduct all screening interviews and actigraphy baseline remotely, reserving PSG and collateral verification for participants who complete an 8-week home sleep diary showing consistent reporting, enabling rapid pre-screening but deferring certainty.

Admit participants on the basis of structured clinical interview and partner history alone, then use the first 2 residential nights (funded as screening rather than study nights) to observe naturalistic sleep before committing to the full protocol, minimizing upfront gatekeeping but accepting facility turnover risk.
review: Stricter screening reduces waste but extends the pre-admission pathway and may exclude genuine parasomnia patients whose events are infrequent or suppressed by travel anxiety; looser screening accelerates flow but the options do not account for the clinical psychologist's time burden in managing participants who must be discharged early due to zero-event stays, nor do they address how month-18 failure triggers (fewer than 25 enrolled or 40 events) will be assessed if admission criteria shift mid-study.

name: Tiered Sensor Acquisition Cadence and Cost Allocation
consequences: Running full PSG (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) every night maximizes data richness but incurs recurring technician setup time, electrode costs, and storage load; the protocol schedules full PSG only on nights 1, mid-stay, and final night plus flagged escalation nights to preserve budget. Reducing PSG frequency saves consumables and labor but risks missing morphological nuance in episodes that occur on low-burden nights, and heightens dependence on dry-electrode and sensor accuracy.
options: Perform scheduled full PSG nightly on a rotating subset of 2–3 of the 8 active suites, ensuring continuous classical polysomnographic coverage while distributing technician load, accepting elevated per-participant sensor cost but gaining dense longitudinal EMG and EEG patterns across the cohort.

Maintain the current protocol (full PSG on nights 1, mid, final, and flagged escalations) but implement accelerated dry-electrode calibration procedures and mattress-sensor fusion preprocessing to extract event morphology from low-burden nights in real time, reducing consumable cost and technician burden at the cost of algorithmic complexity upfront.

Reserve full PSG exclusively for episodes that the technician flags and the first episode per participant, then continue with low-burden monitoring for all subsequent weeks, freeing resources for recruitment and scoring but accepting reduced EMG phenotyping granularity for repeat episodes and inter-person variability.
review: The tiered model assumes that technicians can reliably flag events in real time on low-burden nights (identifying confusional arousals or subtle movement patterns without scalp EEG) and that dry-electrode headbands and mattress sensors will provide sufficient quality for morphology inference; none of the options account for the cost and training overhead of false-positive escalations or the risk that mid-stay PSG nights capture no events, wasting full-montage consumables and technician time.

name: Video Recording Scope and Data Governance
consequences: Bedroom video is the only modality capturing gross motor behavior (walking distance, collision risk, coordination) essential for parasomnia phenotyping, but privacy constraints severely limit public sharing and create ongoing institutional liability and storage burden. Not recording video eliminates this governance risk but loses behavioral context and forces reliance on actigraphy and technician observation, reducing confidence in event classification and severity assessment.
options: Deploy bedroom cameras with automated face-blurring and continuous on-site storage under encrypted archive with no off-site backup, restricting all video access to the PI and night technician, discarding raw video after 6 months once behavioral events are manually annotated in a de-identified event registry, maximizing privacy but preventing post-hoc independent review of adjudication.

Record video only during flagged event windows (triggered by mattress sensor or technician observation), with encrypted storage and restricted access matching current protocol, reducing privacy exposure and storage footprint while preserving behavioral documentation for disputed episodes, though accepting technician detection as a gating filter.

Obtain explicit separate informed consent for video storage and use a two-tiered system: collect full behavioral video during the stay for real-time technician and PI review, then offer participants the option to authorize de-identified behavioral annotation export for future research (without video frames), enabling broader sharing of phenotypic markers while respecting opt-in privacy.
review: Video is ethically essential for safety and behavioral phenotyping in a residential facility, but all options defer the systemic question of how to balance the research utility of video against participants' privacy expectations and institutional liability. The current protocol's explicit statement that 'video is not deposited in public repositories' is a hard governance decision, not a lever; none of the options genuinely negotiate this trade-off—they merely adjust technical scope. A missing option would be partnering with ethics and legal to define conditions (e.g., explicit consent, anonymization standards, benefit-to-privacy ratio) under which a carefully curated anonymized video dataset could be shared with external adjudicators, but this raises regulatory and reputational stakes not addressed in the options.

name: Overnight Staffing Model and Event-Response Coverage
consequences: The current plan assumes 3 technicians rotating nights to cover 8 active suites safely. Staffing at lower ratios (one technician covering more suites) reduces labor costs but increases response time to events and annotation error if a technician is simultaneously setting up PSG or managing a medical incident. Increasing technician FTE improves safety margin and annotation quality but consumes approximately 25–30% of the annual budget; overseas shift models or per-diem contractors reduce fixed cost but introduce training and continuity risk.
options: Maintain the planned 3-technician rotation (1 per night, 8-suite ratio) as the baseline, but hire two additional part-time floaters on a 12-hour on-call contract stationed 30 minutes away, triggered only if two events occur within a 4-hour window or a medical escalation occurs, accepting modest upfront overhead while preserving response capacity and technician well-being.

Reduce core night staffing to 2 technicians and implement a formalized protocol for the clinical psychologist and postdocs to be on-call backup for event annotation and non-medical participant support (reassurance, reorientation after episodes), assuming these senior staff can be reliably mobilized within 30 minutes and have documented orientation to the facility, reducing fixed FTE costs by approximately 15% while accepting professional bleed-over from research roles into clinical support.

Operate a single-technician night model (covering both suites and escalations via video monitoring and remote annotation support, with the PI on-call for medical or safety decisions), outsourcing actigraphy and dry-electrode setup to a daytime technician, reducing overnight labor cost to approximately €180K annually but accepting higher annotation error rates, longer event-escalation latency, and elevated burnout risk for a single overnight operator.
review: Staffing directly impacts safety, data quality, and cost, but the options underestimate the cognitive load on technicians who must simultaneously monitor 8 suites, respond to alarms, set up full PSG mid-event, annotate real time, and manage confused or distressed participants post-episode. The plan assumes 'adequate margin for event response and annotation' without specifying expected concurrent-event frequency (e.g., how many episodes per week) or annotation time per event; if event frequency is higher than modeled, even the 3-technician baseline may be insufficient. None of the options address the training burden (how to onboard technicians to reliable real-time event detection and safety judgment) or how staff turnover or illness is covered.

name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Tool Ambition and Validation Scope
consequences: Developing a multi-modal triage algorithm (integrating EMG, mattress sensor, video motion analysis, and audio) that ranks episodes by confidence could substantially reduce manual review burden (Aim 3), but over-ambitious algorithm design (e.g., attempting disorder classification, not just detection) risks training on small event counts, overfitting, and misrepresenting accuracy when tested on the same cohort. Conversely, a minimalist tool (e.g., threshold-based mattress sensor peaks) is easier to validate but may provide little value over human technician judgment.
options: Design the triage tool to score only event likelihood (probability of any parasomnia-like episode present, using ensemble mattress sensor and audio cues), then separately score each episode's morphologic agreement with prespecified NREM parasomnia criteria (confusional vs. sleepwalking-typical movement patterns), validating both components against dual human scoring and reporting sensitivity, specificity, and agreement separately rather than a single composite 'diagnostic' output, explicitly avoiding any claim of autonomous classification.

Implement a simpler approach: technician-in-the-loop workflow where the algorithm ranks detected nocturnal events by likelihood of being a true parasomnia versus false-positive sensor noise, then provides this ranking as a prioritized review list for the second scorer, validating the ranking concordance against how often the second scorer agrees with the algorithm's ordering, accepting lower detection sensitivity in exchange for clearer deployment and minimal retraining between participants.

Pursue a more ambitious multi-modal fusion model integrating scalp EEG (from scheduled PSG nights and enhanced nights), EMG, video motion vectors, and mattress pressure patterns via a neural-network ensemble, trained on a held-out test set of adjudicated events, reporting cross-validated accuracy and generalization to new participants; accepting increased development time and the inherent risk of overfitting to the small event sample, but potentially unlocking richer morphologic discrimination.
review: Aim 3 explicitly states the goal is to 'reduce manual review burden' and benchmark against dual human scorer agreement, not to claim autonomous diagnosis. The options span realism, but none adequately address the core constraint: if only 70–80% of admitted participants generate at least one captured event and event frequency is typically 1–3 per week per participant, the algorithm training set may comprise 150–300 total adjudicated events across 50–70 participants—a small sample prone to overfitting. The current protocol bans the phrase 'autonomous diagnosis,' which is appropriate, but the options do not discuss how to prevent inadvertent scope creep (e.g., clinicians or referring neurologists requesting disorder-specific classification despite the protocol's methodological limits) or how to communicate algorithm uncertainty transparently to external collaborators or funders.

name: Dataset Curation and Open-Science Sharing Strategy
consequences: Depositing de-identified physiological data (EEG, EOG, EMG, mattress sensor, audio, ambient sensor streams) in a recognized repository (OPENNEURO, PhysioNet) advances reproducibility and maximizes research leverage, but requires careful de-identification, documentation, and licensing. Excluding video entirely (as stated) is protective but forecloses any opportunity for external morphologic validation or secondary video-based phenotyping; strict data use agreements limit adoption by secondary researchers but mitigate misuse risk.
options: Create a fully documented, BIDS-compliant public dataset including all physiological streams (EEG, EOG, EMG, sensor readings) and de-identified event annotations (timestamps, type, duration, manually assigned AASM category), deposited on OPENNEURO without video, with a permissive CC-BY-4.0 license and a supplementary methods paper, accepting broad reuse and potential secondary discoveries while relying on ethical training and journal peer review to deter misuse.

Implement a tiered-access model: place raw de-identified physiological data and event annotations in a public repository, but host a separate secure instance containing motion-derived video descriptors (e.g., pose estimation vectors, activity heatmaps, no identifiable faces or full video frames) accessible only to credentialed researchers under a signed data use agreement, balancing open science with protective governance while enabling advanced secondary research.

Restrict the public dataset to summary statistics, aggregated event distributions by participant subgroup, and a curated subset of representative 30-minute polysomnographic segments from 3–5 exemplar participants, with individual-level raw data available by application to a steering committee, preserving participant privacy and institutional control while accepting slower knowledge diffusion and reduced secondary research momentum.
review: Open science and data sharing accelerate discovery, but the options do not fully navigate the tension between maximizing transparency (depositing raw data immediately post-publication) and managing the risk of secondary researchers misinterpreting sensor noise as events, redefining parasomnia classification without clinical validation, or over-interpreting small subgroup findings. The plan states that de-identified data will be shared but does not specify versioning, documentation depth, or how to handle data corrections post-publication. None of the options address the overhead cost and timeline for preparing publication-quality datasets (cleaning, documentation, BIDS conversion) and the risk that dataset curation delays final publications beyond the 36-month success criterion.

name: Residential Facility Expansion Timeline and Risk Gating
consequences: The plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 suites until the pilot demonstrates safe operations, acceptable data quality, and manageable false-alarm rates. Expanding early increases capacity and per-participant enrollment efficiency but risks scaling safety or operational problems; waiting beyond month 18–24 for a proven model may forfeit recruitment windows and delays secondary-aim analyses. Expansion also hinges on securing additional funding or reallocating year-three budget, which may not be guaranteed.
options: Proceed with expansion to 10 suites (net addition of 2 suite-ready bedrooms) by month 12–14, funded by reallocating €150K from the year-two operations budget and deferring some publication costs, contingent on documented enrollment of at least 20 participants and zero serious safety incidents or unexplained technician annotation failures through month 12, accepting moderate acceleration risk in exchange for higher per-year enrollment velocity and the ability to address month-18 failure triggers proactively.

Maintain the 8-suite baseline through month 18, then make a formal go/no-go decision on expansion based on the external advisory board's 18-month review of enrollment, event-capture rate, data quality, and manuscript progress; if metrics are met, add a third facility (renting an adjacent residential property with 4 suites, operating independently), effectively doubling capacity but accepting the infrastructure overhead of managing two buildings and longer timeline to full 12-suite operation.

Preserve the option for incremental 2-suite expansions but gate each addition on securing dedicated funding (departmental or pharmaceutical partnership) rather than reallocating core operational budget, accepting slower growth and potential under-utilization of infrastructure capacity if partnerships do not materialize, but protecting core operations and publications from expansion-related financial stress.
review: Facility expansion is coupled to recruitment velocity (how quickly participants enroll and produce events) and funding security, but the plan does not specify recruitment pipeline assumptions or timelines. The options assume facilities can be expanded smoothly, but they do not account for the lag between approving expansion, renovating additional suites, training technicians on new spaces, and achieving steady enrollment. If the early cohort (months 0–6) enrolls slowly or produces lower-than-expected event yields, expansion becomes both a financial drain and a reputational signal of ambition outpacing execution. The month-18 external review is a natural gating point, but by then recruitment cycles may be entrenched; no option addresses how to dynamically adjust recruitment targets (e.g., accelerating referrals from the Deutsche Schlafforschung network) if enrollment lags by month 6–9.

name: Admission Pathway Entry Criteria and Collateral Verification Stringency
consequences: Stricter collateral verification (requiring confirmed bed-partner or household-member contact with documented parasomnia history) reduces false admissions and ensures captured events align with genuine clinical presentation; however, it narrows the recruitment pool in single-living participants and lengthens the pre-screening workup. Conversely, lighter collateral requirements accelerate enrollment but may admit participants whose reported symptoms do not manifest during residence, consuming 2–8 week observation slots without generating usable event data.
options: Require independent collateral witness interview conducted by clinical psychologist before admission, with documented episodic history and injury-risk assessment recorded in structured form and retained in study records

Accept self-reported symptom history alone with a single screening polysomnography session performed within 6 weeks pre-admission to confirm at least one captured episode or unequivocal parasomnia marker

Implement a graded collateral pathway: participants with available household witness proceed to standard admission; those without collateral receive enhanced pre-screening video call with two independent sleep physicians reviewing symptom description and prior medical records before approval
review: Requiring live collateral ensures clinical confidence but excludes isolated adults, individuals with privacy concerns about bed partners, and divorced or separated participants—populations often represented in sleep disorder clinics. The options neglect that bed partners themselves may harbor denial, misattribution, or fear of the affected individual seeking formal evaluation, making collateral accounts unreliable as sole gates to admission.

name: Within-Stay Extension Protocol and Event-Capture Contingency Trigger
consequences: A pre-defined extension pathway (additional 1–2 weeks for participants with zero captured events after initial 2–8 week stay, capped at 10 weeks total) increases the probability of observing low-frequency episodes and strengthens Aim 2 phenotyping; it also consumes facility capacity and extends participant burden and compensation costs. A ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering recruitment-criteria review provides an early-warning gate but requires tracking event-yield metrics continuously and may force premature shifts in inclusion criteria before sufficient sample accumulates to judge true event frequency.
options: Offer extension eligibility only to participants in the first two cohorts (months 1–12), with strict documentation of attempted-event circumstances; discontinue extension after month 12 to preserve facility availability for new recruitment rounds and assess true event-yield under standard stay duration

Implement automatic extension for any participant with fewer than one adjudicated event by the end of standard stay, with participant and family consent, but cap cumulative extensions at 6 weeks per participant and track extension outcomes as a separate cohort for separate analysis of 'delayed responders'

Establish extension eligibility based on objectively detectable precursor markers (e.g., elevated arousal indices, sleep fragmentation patterns, or high-frequency subclinical microarousals detected on enhanced nights) rather than zero-event triggers, aiming to preselect extension candidates with higher a priori event probability
review: Arbitrary extension protocols risk habituation—participants extended beyond 8 weeks may suppress parasomnia expression due to environmental familiarity or anticipatory anxiety. None of the options account for the fact that a genuinely low-frequency parasomnia (1–2 episodes per month self-reported) may still yield zero captures in a 10-week observation window due to random variation, creating false negatives that masquerade as inclusion-criterion failures.

name: Real-Time Technician Event-Flagging Sensitivity and Escalation Thresholds
consequences: High-sensitivity night-technician flagging of suspected episodes for same-night or next-morning full PSG escalation maximizes capture resolution but generates false alarms, depleting the escalation-PSG budget and technician cognitive load, while low-sensitivity flagging preserves budget but misses low-amplitude or ambiguous episodes that dual scorers might adjudicate as events. The balance directly affects event yield and the cost-per-adjudicated-event ratio.
options: Train technicians to flag any sustained arousal, movement cluster, or vocalization lasting >20 seconds followed by brief awakening or sleep stage shift, triggering automatic PSG escalation on the following night, accepting ~40–60% false alarm rate in exchange for comprehensive event capture

Establish a three-tier flagging scale (definite parasomnia, probable parasomnia, uncertain/artifact) and deploy escalation PSG only for 'definite' flags, with probabilistic sampling of 'probable' flags at a controlled rate (e.g., 30% random sample) to balance budget and representativeness

Implement hybrid flagging: technicians flag suspected events in real time, but flagging decisions are reviewed within 2 hours by the on-call senior researcher (postdoc or PI by phone consultation) before PSG escalation is confirmed, introducing a human gate but adding logistical coordination complexity
review: All options assume technicians can reliably distinguish parasomnia episodes from other arousal phenomena (sleep apnea micro-awakenings, periodic leg movements, sleep-related hypoventilation events) on the basis of video and audio alone. In reality, clinically naïve technicians often conflate these categories, and retraining variability across a 3-year overnight staff rotation is substantial. None of the options establish inter-rater reliability checks on technician flagging accuracy itself.

name: Overnight Staffing Rotation Structure and Night-Team Composition
consequences: A fixed three-technician rotation covering 8 active suites with 1.5–2 technicians per night ensures adequate event-response coverage and real-time annotation continuity; however, it locks in high personnel costs (approximately €700–800K over 3 years) and creates scheduling fragility if any technician leaves or becomes ill. Conversely, a pool of 4–5 on-call technicians working flexible shifts (2–3 per night, filling gaps via on-call bonus pay) lowers baseline salary but increases no-show risk, quality inconsistency, and technician burnout from unpredictable scheduling.
options: Maintain a dedicated 3-person core team on strict weekly rotation (Mon–Thu, Thu–Sun, Sun–Wed) with a fixed fourth technician hired as a part-time floater (50% FTE) available for illness cover and vacation, supplemented by the clinical psychologist rotating one night per week to provide mental-health safety oversight and relieve pure monitoring burden

Hire 5 sleep technicians (4.5 FTE combined) structured as two overlapping pairs plus one part-time backup, with each pair working 2–3 consecutive weeks of nights, then 2–3 weeks off-shift, allowing deeper post-night recovery and reducing cumulative fatigue while maintaining continuity of inter-technician knowledge transfer

Engage the University Hospital Bonn's existing overnight nursing and EEG-technician pool to co-staff research nights at a negotiated rate (50–60% cost share), leveraging institutional infrastructure and reducing turnover risk; implement a formal service-level agreement specifying minimum response times and QA standards, with hospital staff trained and credentialed as research technicians
review: All three options assume that technician retention and satisfaction are primarily driven by scheduling predictability and fatigue mitigation. In reality, overnight sleep-center work carries high burnout due to social isolation, inversion of circadian rhythms, and emotional burden of witnessing severe parasomnia episodes without clinical authority to intervene. None of the options address retention drivers such as career advancement pathways, mentorship, or peer community—factors that may prove more valuable than shift structure alone.

name: Matched Control Recruitment and Sampling Density
consequences: A paired 1:1 control-to-case ratio (recruiting matched healthy controls for roughly 50–70 NREM parasomnia cases) provides robust normative data for sensor false-positive benchmarking and event-triage tool validation but doubles participant recruitment burden and facility occupancy. A lower ratio (e.g., 1 control per 3–4 cases) reduces recruitment effort and facility load but may leave event-detection algorithms undertrained on normal sleep variability, leading to unquantified generalization risk.
options: Recruit matched controls (age, sex, sleep duration phenotype, and caffeine/alcohol use patterns documented by structured questionnaire) in a strict 1:1 ratio with cases, conduct identical 2-week residential stays with the full enhanced-PSG and sensor battery, and use control data to establish sensor noise profiles and false-alarm thresholds before finalizing the event-triage algorithm

Recruit a smaller (n=15–20) control cohort early (months 3–9) to establish baseline sensor and arousal patterns, then use that cohort's pooled data as a static normative reference for all subsequent case comparisons, avoiding the need for matched pairs and reducing recruitment overhead

Pursue a 'naturalistic control' strategy by enrolling willing cohabitants or family members of parasomnia participants for a single 1-week residential observation using identical sensors but no enhanced PSG, providing cost-effective baseline comparisons while potentially improving recruitment via family bundling
review: Matched controls imply a stable definition of 'healthy sleep'—but healthy control cohorts inevitably contain subclinical sleep pathology (undiagnosed sleep apnea, periodic movements, partial arousals, sleep-stage fragmentation) that mimics parasomnia-adjacent sensor signatures. None of the options account for the fact that screening controls for the absence of any sleep pathology would require pre-admission diagnostic PSG, inflating recruitment burden and potentially creating a 'supernormal' control cohort that does not reflect population sleep variability.

name: Annotation Workflow Parallelization and Independent-Scorer Deployment Cadence
consequences: Deploying two independent raters scoring events immediately after each night (while data are fresh and context is recent) ensures inter-rater reliability checks early and enables rapid algorithm refinement; however, it requires paying two independent scorers (typically postdocs or senior technicians at approximately €150–200 per night per scorer) across all 180+ observation nights over 3 years, adding approximately €60–70K in direct annotation costs. Deferring independent scoring until the final 12 months pools all work but divorces scoring from real-time data context and delays algorithm validation and Aim 3 publication timelines.
options: Implement immediate dual scoring (both scorers review each night's flagged events within 48 hours) for all enhanced-PSG and escalation nights (approximately 50–60% of total observation nights), with single technician scoring applied to low-burden routine nights; track inter-rater agreement continuously and use discordant cases for adjudication discussion by the PI

Deploy a rolling two-person independent-scorer team (postdoc + hired senior technician) who begin scoring after the first 20 participant admissions are complete, ensuring algorithm development time is not bottlenecked; schedule scoring in 2-week blocks (scoring weeks alternating with technician shifts) to allow scorers to perform other research duties and prevent annotation burnout

Establish a remote independent-scoring consortium with 2–3 external sleep-medicine physicians (collaborators at University of Cologne, Max Planck Institute, or German sleep society colleagues) who score events asynchronously on a shared secure portal, compensated per-event at a modest rate (€30–50 per adjudicated event), reducing burden on local staff but introducing geographic distance and potential delays in adjudication and feedback loops
review: All options assume sufficient inter-rater reliability can be achieved with AASM parasomnia scoring criteria alone. In reality, parasomnia events—especially confusional arousals and borderline sleep terrors—exhibit substantial subjective interpretation variance (threshold for 'vocalization,' degree of consciousness, episode duration onset/offset). None of the options include a pre-study rater training and reliability certification protocol (e.g., consensus scoring of 20 gold-standard events before independent work begins) or periodic recalibration checks.

name: Phased Facility Expansion and Property Lease-vs.-Purchase Decision
consequences: Keeping the initial 8-suite facility and deferring expansion to an alternate property (if uptake justifies it) minimizes upfront capital risk and preserves flexibility; however, it caps enrollment velocity and may force a slowdown in Aim 2 phenotyping if demand exceeds 8-suite capacity after month 18. Conversely, renovating the facility's latent 12-suite capacity now (investing an additional €400–500K in year one) enables faster enrollment scaling but locks in renovation costs and facility overhead (utilities, cleaning, maintenance) before event-yield and participant retention patterns are proven.
options: Execute renovations for only the initial 8 suites in year one; conduct a formal capacity-planning review at month 18 that examines enrollment demand, event-yield rates, and available budget; if demand justifies expansion, lease or acquire a second nearby property (e.g., another residential building) rather than densifying a single facility, preserving the domestic feel across distributed sites

Partially renovate a second 4-suite wing (tier B, using lighter soundproofing and sensor infrastructure to reduce costs) in parallel with the main 8-suite launch (months 1–4 of year one), bringing it online by month 6 for contingent use; activate B only if month-6 enrollment and retention metrics exceed 80% of projected targets, otherwise leave B in standby mode to minimize ongoing overhead

Contract with a backup residential property owner in Bonn to maintain a pre-signed option agreement to lease and rapidly outfit an additional 4–6 suites within 60 days if enrollment demand and event-yield metrics trigger expansion at month 15; keep the backup property in 'ready' status via quarterly site visits and pre-negotiated renovation scopes, accepting modest option-fee costs in exchange for flexibility
review: All options assume that expansion is fundamentally constrained by capital availability and facility footprint. In practice, the binding constraint may be recruitment capacity—the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin referral network and University Hospital Bonn's sleep clinic may not generate 40+ new NREM parasomnia referrals per year even if facility slots are available. None of the options explore whether expansion should instead be conditional on demonstrating predictable referral pipelines and participant yield, rather than facility readiness.

name: Recruitment Geographic Footprint and Specialist-Referral Concentration
consequences: Narrowing recruitment to Bonn-based and nearby Rhineland neurology referral networks raises the proportion of pre-screened, specialty-confirmed parasomnia cases and lowers no-show risk; broader national or international recruitment increases event-yield diversity but introduces travel fatigue, protocol deviation risk, and coordination overhead. The core tension is whether to optimize for statistical event capture at the cost of operational simplicity and staff burden.
options: Restrict active recruitment to University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and three contracted regional neurology practices within 50 km, with explicit staffing allocation for local pre-admission interviews and bed-partner collateral verification.

Establish a joint intake pathway with the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin allowing referrals from any affiliated center across Germany, with telephone and telehealth pre-screening to reduce in-person travel burden on remote participants.

Launch a dual-tier enrollment model: prioritize Bonn-local specialists for the first 20 admissions to build site reputation and operational stability, then expand nationally in year two if event-yield targets are met.
review: Specialist referral concentration accelerates case confirmation but creates dependency on a small number of gatekeepers; if a referral practice shifts protocols or loses a key clinician, recruitment pipeline narrows abruptly. None of the options address retention risk if a referred participant experiences a minor adverse event early—word-of-mouth among regional sleep clinics can devastate enrollment momentum.

name: Technician Shift-Coverage Architecture and Night-Rotation Sustainability
consequences: Rotating three technicians across 8 active suites requires each technician to work 1 in 3 nights; this maintains legally mandated break periods but creates individual fatigue vulnerability and inconsistent annotation quality. Hiring a fourth technician eases workload but increases payroll; conversely, implementing block scheduling (e.g., three consecutive nights then six off) reduces context-switching but concentrates fatigue and increases scheduling inflexibility. The critical dependency is sustainable annotation accuracy across 24/7 coverage without burnout-driven quality drift.
options: Maintain the planned three-technician rotation (one per night) with mandatory peer-review of night-time event annotations during day shifts, acceptance of ±10% inter-night variance in flagging sensitivity as normal operational variation, and quarterly burnout screening to trigger shift-model review.

Hire a fourth technician to reduce shift frequency to 1 in 4 nights per technician, allocating the additional €120K–€150K annual cost from contingency or by reducing participant compensation by €10 per night to lower operating cost elasticity.

Implement a hybrid model: deploy two technicians simultaneously on nights with high event-probability participants (e.g., after week one of a stay when event frequency is higher) and accept single technician coverage on baseline nights, with asynchronous scoring of video review queued for daytime independent raters.
review: Rotation architecture trades technician autonomy and annotation consistency against budget and scheduling flexibility; even with peer review, a single technician's idiosyncratic event flagging bias accumulates undetected over months if the same technician reviews their own night-flagged events. Block scheduling and rotation models alike assume technicians will self-report fatigue—many will not, creating silent quality degradation.

name: Facility Liability Containment and Injury-Event Disclosure Policy
consequences: Residential monitoring inevitably produces occasional minor incidents—brief falls, collision with furniture, emotional distress during parasomnia—that require incident documentation, participant notification, and potential ethics board reporting. Transparent disclosure to participants at admission and in consent materials protects ethics standing but may suppress enrollment from risk-averse candidates. Restrictive incident thresholds (reporting only serious injury) reduce administrative burden but invite regulatory audit risk and reputational damage if undisclosed incidents surface later.
options: Adopt a zero-threshold incident policy: all non-trivial events (including near-falls, property contact, and emotional distress episodes) are documented within 24 hours, reviewed by the clinical psychologist, disclosed to participants verbally and in writing, and reported to the ethics board with quarterly summaries; frame this as transparent safety culture in all recruitment materials.

Establish incident severity tiers: report only events requiring clinical intervention (laceration, suspected fracture, acute distress requiring pharmacologic intervention) to the ethics board and participant; minor contact events are logged internally and aggregated quarterly for trend analysis without individual participant notification unless requested.

Require all participants to sign a high-risk acknowledgment before admission stating that residential monitoring of parasomnia involves inherent fall and collision risk despite safety modifications, limiting facility liability for minor incidents and deferring incident disclosure to ethics-board-mandated reporting only.
review: Liability containment and disclosure transparency are in direct opposition: zero-threshold transparency may discourage enrollment and inflate administrative overhead, while high-threshold reporting creates latent regulatory exposure and erodes participant autonomy to know their own risk. None of the options address the situation where a participant sustains a serious injury from equipment failure (e.g., mattress sensor malfunction contributing to falls) rather than parasomnia—liability attribution becomes ambiguous and contested.

name: Public Data Deposition Scope and Metadata Richness for Secondary Research
consequences: Depositing de-identified EEG, sensor streams, and limited behavioral metadata in a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro) amplifies scientific impact and attracts secondary analysis collaborators; withholding data minimizes reputational and privacy-legal risk and preserves competitive advantage for primary-team publications. The tension is between open science credibility and operational/legal defensibility: more public data increases scrutiny of methods and annotation quality but also the probability of discovering methodological flaws or annotation inconsistencies before the team publishes them.
options: Deposit polysomnography montages, dry-electrode EEG headband streams, mattress pressure and respiratory sensor data, ambient audio spectrograms (removing speech-frequency content), time-synchronized event onset and offset annotations, and participant demographics on OpenNeuro within 18 months post-enrollment with a standard BIDS data use agreement; withhold all camera imagery and raw audio transcripts.

Delay public deposition until 24 months after facility closure, allowing the primary team exclusive publication window; then deposit the same EEG and sensor streams with a 12-month embargo on secondary analyses to protect the team's intervention-trial grant applications in phase two.

Create a tiered-access model: deposit summary statistics, aggregate sensor-derived features (e.g., hourly arousal burden, movement density), and event timestamps on OpenNeuro immediately; require application and ethics-board review for access to raw EEG and sensor streams, administered through the University of Bonn research office.
review: Public deposition accelerates reproducibility but invites immediate critique of annotation quality and event-classification criteria before the team's own validation studies are published; a secondary analyst may reprocess the data and publish contradictory findings, damaging the unit's credibility. None of the options address the scenario where an external group identifies annotation errors in the deposited dataset—the team's publications citing those annotations become partially invalidated, and retractions or corrections become necessary.

name: Extended-Stay Eligibility Criteria and Repetitive Event-Capture Targeting
consequences: Participants with zero captured events after the initial 2–8 week stay may be offered an extension up to 10 weeks total under the protocol ceiling; extending low-event participants improves individual phenotyping but creates staffing and facility resource strain. Tightening inclusion criteria ex ante (e.g., requiring ≥4 self-reported episodes per month instead of ≥2) reduces zero-event admissions but may exclude genuine low-frequency parasomnia cases, biasing the final cohort toward frequent episodics and reducing clinical diversity.
options: Maintain the current 2–8 week baseline stay with optional extension to 10 weeks for zero-event participants, but gate extensions on explicit consent and clinical psychologist approval; use extension data to pilot a sub-protocol examining low-frequency episode triggers, with clear sample-stratification in analysis to avoid conflating baseline and extended-cohort capture rates.

Implement a dynamic termination rule: if no events are captured by week 4 despite confirmed incident history, offer participants immediate discharge with full compensation and optional re-enrollment in a second admission phase 6–12 months later, reducing resource waste and allowing the team to rotate cohort more rapidly.

Establish a pre-admission electroencephalography sleep study requirement (two-night PSG at outpatient center) to confirm parasomnia markers in sleep architecture; use baseline sleep metrics to stratify participants by predicted event-yield probability before admission, accepting only candidates with polysomnographic features suggestive of parasomnia likelihood.
review: Extended stays improve individual capture but introduce selection bias: participants with zero events who consent to extension differ psychologically and physiologically from those who decline, creating a hidden subgroup. The protocol ceiling of 10 weeks total is arbitrary—why not 12, or 16?—and the 20% unproductive-admission threshold before review triggers is vulnerable to gaming: if 19% of admissions yield zero events, the team continues unchanged despite potential systematic under-ascertainment.

name: Semi-Automated Event-Triage Model Development Checkpoint and Reporting Honesty
consequences: The protocol commits to developing and benchmarking a semi-automated triage tool against dual human scorer agreement, framing it as a labor-reduction aid rather than an autonomous diagnostic system. However, the team faces pressure to publish positive algorithm results in high-impact venues; if the triage model's sensitivity or specificity lags human scorers, the team may be tempted to selectively report confidence intervals, train on subset data that inflates performance, or redefine the success metric. Early publication of overstated algorithmic claims risks credibility damage and downstream regulatory scrutiny if the tool is ever commercialized.
options: Establish an independent algorithm-validation panel (two external sleep medicine physicians and one biostatistician) who receive raw algorithm outputs, human scorer labels, and confusion matrices before the team submits any publication; require the panel to co-author or formally endorse the performance claims, creating accountability for honest reporting of sensitivity, specificity, and failure modes.

Commit in the protocol amendment to pre-registering the triage model's performance targets (e.g., sensitivity ≥80% against dual human consensus) on a public registry (e.g., Open Science Framework) before any algorithm development begins; any deviation from the registered target must be explained in the publication and flagged as exploratory.

Adopt a deliberate under-claim strategy: report the algorithm's agreement with dual human consensus (rather than claiming sensitivity or specificity) and explicitly characterize failure modes (e.g., confusion between confusional arousals and polysomnographic artifacts); position the tool as a preliminary annotation-reduction prototype requiring further validation rather than a mature triage instrument.
review: Algorithm validation pressures the team to balance open-science honesty against publication incentives; even well-intentioned researchers experience confirmation bias in feature selection and performance metric choice. None of the options directly address the scenario where the triage model performs worse than a simpler baseline rule (e.g., 'any arousal + movement in the same epoch'), forcing the team to either suppress the result or publish a negative finding that damages career prospects and future funding.

name: Facility Expansion Trigger Conditions and Deferred-Growth Financial Planning
consequences: The protocol defers expansion from 8 to 12 suites pending proof of operational sustainability, acceptable false-alarm rates, and data quality. If triggered, expansion requires €500K–€700K in renovation and equipment; failure to trigger leaves unused facility capacity and misallocated capital. The ambiguity lies in defining 'acceptable data quality' and 'false alarm rates' before expansion—the team may face internal disagreement about whether to expand with borderline metrics, creating organizational gridlock and wasted facility overhead.
options: Define explicit numeric gates ex ante: expand only if (a) ≥40 adjudicated parasomnia events are captured in the first 25 admissions, (b) video-annotated false alarm rate (non-parasomnia events incorrectly flagged by technician) is <15%, and (c) inter-rater agreement on event classification is ≥80%; publish the gates in the protocol and conduct a blinded external review at month 18 to determine expansion authorization.

Plan expansion as a staged discretionary option: maintain 8-suite operations through year 2, collect operational and cost data, and make a year-3 expansion decision based on actual enrollment velocity and budget surplus; treat potential 12-suite capacity as optionality rather than a committed trajectory, reducing financial planning rigidity.

Establish a pre-committed financing agreement with the University Hospital Bonn real-estate and clinical-operations divisions: secure a locked renovation quote and equipment lease terms valid through month 24, with the option to activate expansion without re-negotiation if operational gates are met; structure this as a non-binding letter of intent to reduce administrative burden and decision latency.
review: Expansion gates provide clarity but lock the team into predetermined success metrics that may not reflect actual scientific utility: capturing 40 events with 80% inter-rater agreement is numerically measurable, but clinical and statistical heterogeneity within those 40 events might render the sample inadequate for Aim 2 phenotyping. The 15% false-alarm threshold is not grounded in any prior reference standard. None of the options account for sunk-cost bias: if expansion financing is already committed by year 1, the team may rationalize expansion regardless of actual metrics to avoid admitting waste.
